items.front: Where must current altimeter settings be obtained from?
items.back: Direct-reading instruments or directly from weather reporting stations.

items.front: What should ATC do if a pilot requests the altimeter setting in millibars?
items.back: Ask the nearest weather reporting station for the equivalent millibar setting.

items.front: When is the term Estimated Altimeter used by USAF and USA controllers?
items.back: When the altimeter setting being reported or received is estimated.

items.front: What two direct sources are authorized for current altimeter settings under 2-7-1?
items.back: Direct-reading instruments and direct contact with weather reporting stations.

items.front: When must terminal controllers identify the source of an altimeter setting?
items.back: When the setting is for a location other than the aircraft's departure or destination airport.

items.front: When must en route controllers identify the source of an altimeter setting?
items.back: Whenever they issue an altimeter setting.

items.front: How is an altimeter setting issued if it is more than one hour old?
items.back: State the facility name, the altimeter setting, and the words more than one hour old.

items.front: How often must en route aircraft receive an altimeter setting while in your area of jurisdiction?
What is the general terminal rule for issuing altimeter settings to departures?
items.back: Issue the altimeter to all departures, except for approved local-operator and scheduled-air-carrier omissions.

items.front: When may the tower omit the altimeter for an arriving aircraft?
items.back: When approach control with jurisdiction has already sequenced or vectored the aircraft to the airport.

items.front: What extra altimeter-setting action is required for a nonprecision approach when the weather report says pressure falling rapidly?
items.back: Issue altimeter changes as frequently as practical in addition to the initial-contact altimeter.

items.front: What altimeter setting is used when clearing an aircraft below the lowest usable flight level?
items.back: The nearest weather reporting station to the point where the aircraft will descend below that flight level.

items.front: When do high barometric pressure procedures apply?
items.back: When the barometric pressure is greater than 31.00 inches mercury.

items.front: What are en route and arriving pilots told to do with the 31.00 setting during high barometric pressure procedures?
items.back: Leave the altimeter set to 31.00 until reaching the final approach fix.

items.front: What are departures told to do with the 31.00 setting during high barometric pressure procedures?
items.back: Set 31.00 prior to reaching any mandatory or crossing altitude or 1,500 feet AGL, whichever is lower.

items.front: What must affected ARTCCs do when pressure is above or forecast to be above 31.00 inches mercury?
items.back: Request a high barometric pressure NOTAM through the U.S. NOTAM Office.

items.front: Why can the cockpit altimeter and Mode C display differ during high barometric pressure procedures?
items.back: Because the transponder reports pressure altitude while ATC automation applies an altimeter setting to display altitude above MSL.
